rule.desc: Use ES modules (`import`/`export`) exclusively. Avoid CommonJS `require`/`module.exports`.
rule.rationale: ES modules are the standard, enable tree-shaking, provide static analysis, and are supported everywhere TypeScript targets.

rule.desc: Export only what consumers need. Use named exports over default exports.
rule.rationale: Named exports enable better IDE auto-imports, refactoring support, and tree-shaking. Default exports lose the name binding and complicate re-exports.

example.code: // Avoid - default export export default function loadConfig() { ... }

rule.desc: Use barrel files (`index.ts`) sparingly for package boundaries. Avoid deep barrel nesting.
rule.rationale: Barrel files define clean public APIs at package boundaries but cause bundle bloat and circular dependency risk when overused internally.

rule.desc: Use `import type` for imports used only in type positions.
rule.rationale: Type-only imports are erased at compile time, reducing bundle size and preventing circular dependency issues at runtime.

rule.desc: Dependencies flow inward: infrastructure depends on domain, never the reverse.
rule.rationale: Inward-pointing dependencies keep domain logic portable and testable, independent of frameworks and I/O.

rule.desc: Use path aliases in `tsconfig.json` for cross-cutting imports instead of deep relative paths.
rule.rationale: Path aliases improve readability and make refactoring (moving files) safer. Deep relative paths (`../../../`) are fragile and hard to read.

example.code: // Bad - deep relative path import { logger } from "../../../shared/logger.js";
